stem: A population has mean \( \mu=[[mu]] \) and standard deviation \( \sigma=[[sigma]] \). What is the z-score of an individual value \( x=[[x]] \), i.e. \( z=\dfrac{x-\mu}{\sigma} \)?
distractor_rationales: This flips the sign, computing \( \mu - x \) instead of \( x - \mu \).
This inverts the z-score formula.
This divides by \( \mu \) instead of \( \sigma \) in the denominator.
This adds \( x \) and \( \mu \) instead of subtracting them.
This is off by one from the correct z-score.
This is off by one from the correct z-score.

stem: A population has mean \( \mu=[[mu]] \) and standard deviation \( \sigma=[[sigma]] \). A random sample of size \( n=[[n]] \) is drawn, with sample mean \( \bar{x}=[[xbar]] \). By the Central Limit Theorem, the sampling distribution of \( \bar{x} \) is approximately normal with standard error \( \sigma/\sqrt{n} \). What is the z-score of \( \bar{x} \), i.e. \( z = \dfrac{\bar{x}-\mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} \)?
solution: The standard error is \( \sigma/\sqrt{n} = [[sigma]]/[[sqn]] \), so \( z = \dfrac{\bar{x}-\mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} = \dfrac{[[d]]\cdot[[sqn]]}{[[sigma]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( \sigma \) itself as the standard error, forgetting to divide by \( \sqrt{n} \).
This multiplies \( \sigma \) by \( \sqrt{n} \) instead of dividing, inverting the standard-error formula.
This uses \( n \) instead of \( \sqrt{n} \) in the standard error.
This flips the sign of the z-score.
This is off by one from the correct z-score.
This is off by one from the correct z-score.

stem: A population has mean \( \mu=[[mu]] \) and standard deviation \( \sigma=[[sigma]] \). A value \( x \) has z-score \( z=\dfrac{[[z_n]]}{[[z_d]]} \). What is the value of \( x \)?
distractor_rationales: This subtracts \( z\sigma \) instead of adding it.
This multiplies \( \mu \) by \( z\sigma \) instead of adding them.
This is just \( z\sigma \), forgetting to add \( \mu \).
This inverts the z-score fraction before scaling by \( \sigma \).
This is off by one from the correct raw score.
This is off by one from the correct raw score.

stem: In a population with standard deviation \( \sigma=[[sigma]] \), two values are \( x_1=[[x1]] \) and \( x_2=[[x2]] \). How many standard deviations apart are \( x_1 \) and \( x_2 \), that is, what is \( \dfrac{x_2-x_1}{\sigma} \)?
distractor_rationales: This flips the sign, computing \( x_1-x_2 \) instead of \( x_2-x_1 \).
This inverts the correct ratio.
This multiplies by \( \sigma \) instead of dividing by it.
This adds \( x_1 \) and \( x_2 \) instead of subtracting them.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: A population has standard deviation \( \sigma=[[sigma]] \). A random sample's mean differs from the population mean by \( \bar{x}-\mu=[[diff]] \), which corresponds to a z-score of \( z=\dfrac{[[z_n]]}{[[z_d]]} \) under the Central Limit Theorem \( \left(z=\dfrac{\bar{x}-\mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}\right) \). What sample size \( n \) was used?
distractor_rationales: This finds \( \sqrt{n} \) but forgets to square it to get \( n \).
This inverts the ratio before squaring.
This inverts the z-score fraction before squaring.
This is off by one from the correct sample size.
This is off by one from the correct sample size.
This swaps the roles of \( \sigma \) and the mean difference.
